title-group.article-title: Selenium enrichment enhances the alleviating effect of Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG on alcoholic liver injury in mice

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Institute of Food Science and Technology, College of Biological Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, PR China. xucong1154@163.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Selenium-enriched probiotics have attracted much attention due to the physiological activities of both probiotics and selenium (organic selenium). In this study, we investigated the mitigating effect of selenium-enriched Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG@Se) and its pathway on alcohol-induced liver injury (ALI) in mice. The results showed that LGG@Se was superior to LGG and sodium selenite in alleviating ALI. Oral LGG@Se effectively prevented lipid metabolism disorders and liver oxidative damage in mice caused by excessive alcohol intake. 16S amplicon sequencing showed that LGG@Se intervention increased the abundance of beneficial bacteria and suppressed the growth of harmful bacteria in the intestinal tract of over-drinking mice, and thus effectively modulated the homeostasis of intestinal flora, which were highly correlated with the improvement of liver function. Liver metabolomics analysis indicated that LGG@Se intervention altered liver metabolic profiling, and the characteristic biomarkers were mainly involved in amino acid metabolism, including alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism, arginine biosynthesis, etc. In addition, LGG@Se intervention modulated the expression of genes and proteins related to lipid metabolism and oxidative stress in liver of over-drinking mice. Western blot analysis revealed that LGG@Se intervention up-regulated the expression of intestinal barrier function-related proteins, thereby ameliorating alcohol-induced intestinal barrier damage. Collectively, these findings provide scientific evidence that LGG@Se possesses the biological activity of improving alcohol-induced lipid metabolism and intestinal microbiota disorder.

list-item.p: Selenium enrichment enhances hepatoprotective effect of Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG.
Selenium-enriched Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG prevents lipid metabolism disorder.
Selenium-enriched Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG prevents liver oxidative stress damage.
Selenium-enriched Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG alters intestinal microbiota.
Selenium-enriched Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG ameliorates intestinal barrier damage.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: As one of the most beloved drinks in the world, alcoholic beverages are consumed by billions of people around the world for centuries. Alcohol-induced liver injury (ALI) has become an increasingly serious health problem, mainly due to liver damage caused by alcohol abuse (Kim et al., 2023). The World Health Organisation (WHO) has reported that more than 40% of liver diseases each year are attributed to alcohol abuse (Asrani et al., 2021), with mortality rates significantly higher than those for diabetes, tuberculosis and acquired immunodeficiency syndrome. The formation mechanism of alcoholic liver disease is complex and involves multiple factors, mainly including ethanol-induced hepatocyte damage, liver oxidative stress, and inflammatory responses (Wu et al., 2023). It is widely accepted that alcoholism may lead to excessive accumulation of triglycerides and lipid peroxidation in hepatocytes, which in turn causes oxidative stress damage and inflammation in the liver (Hartmann et al., 2012). In addition, alcoholism not only affects the composition of intestinal microflora, but also destroys the intestinal barrier function and mucosal integrity (Bajaj, 2019). Impairment of the intestinal mucosal barrier and increased abundance of gram-negative bacteria often lead to a massive transfer of enteric endotoxins to the liver portal vein, delivering a "second blow" to the liver, and thus exacerbating the pathological process of liver disease (Liu et al., 2022). So far, there is no clear explanation for the pathogenesis of ALI, but intestinal flora and liver oxidative stress have been shown to be associated with the occurrence of ALI (Wang et al., 2019). Therefore, ALI can be ameliorated by regulating the composition of intestinal flora, improving liver lipid metabolism and oxidative stress. To date, in addition to alcohol abstinence and liver transplantation, effective therapeutic strategies for the prevention of alcoholic liver disease remain to be determined.

Probiotics are a class of live microorganisms that are beneficial to the health of their hosts. In recent years, the functions of probiotics have received more and more attention, and their research and application are becoming hotspots in the field of food science, with a view to developing a wide range of probiotic products to contribute to the promotion of human health. The effectiveness of probiotics in enhancing alcohol metabolism, as well as in preventing and treating alcoholic liver damage has been previously demonstrated (Imani Fooladi et al., 2013). In particular, Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium have been proven to effectively reduce alcohol-induced liver damage by enhancing intestinal barrier function, restoring intestinal flora balance and resisting oxidative stress in liver (Forsyth et al., 2009). For example, oral administration of Lactobacillus plantarum ZY08 prevented alcoholic liver damage by regulating the expression of lipogenesis-related genes, relieving hepatic oxidative stress and restoring intestinal flora homeostasis (Ding et al., 2022). As a gastric acid-resistant and bile salt-resistant lactobacillus, Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) is often used as a probiotic in commercial functional foods and pharmaceuticals (Wang et al., 2023). It has been reported that LGG has the ability to adhere and colonize the intestine, and oral administration of LGG can attenuate alcohol-induced liver injury through decreasing hepatic liver fatty acid metabolism, ameliorating intestinal mucosal barrier, and regulating intestinal flora homeostasis (Forsyth et al., 2009).

As one of the important trace elements in human body, selenium (Se) plays an irreplaceable role in maintaining human health. Appropriate supplementation of selenium can effectively improve antioxidant and anti-inflammatory efficacy, regulate intestinal barrier and immune response (Zhai et al., 2018). Notably, the bioavailability and bioactivity of organic Se are obviously higher than that of inorganic Se, while the toxicity of organic Se is lower than that of inorganic Se (Yang and Yang, 2023). Many probiotics have the ability to convert toxic inorganic selenium into safer and more effective organic selenium and selenium nanoparticles (SeNPs). Besides, organic Se transformed by probiotics exhibited excellent biosafety, bioavailability and bioactivity (Sun et al., 2022). With the dual effects of Se and probiotics, Se-enriched probiotics have significant effects on human health and have a broad marketing potential in the development of food that contributes to health. In the last decade, as an important source of organic Se, Se-enriched probiotics have received more and more attention in the world due to their dual health benefits of Se and probiotics (Chen et al., 2022). Biogenic Se nanoparticles obtained from L. casei ATCC 393 was previously reported to mitigate the intestinal barrier dysfunction induced by diquat (Qiao et al., 2020). Probiotics rich in Se have been demonstrated to have a range of health benefits for the host, including anti-oxidant, anti-inflammatory and cancer-preventive effects (Pophaly et al., 2014). Selenium deficiency is associated with liver injury in alcoholics, probably due to lipid peroxidation secondary to reduced activity of selenase glutathione peroxidase (Dworkin et al., 1988). Although the physiological efficacy of Se-enriched probiotics has been widely reported, their ameliorative effects and mechanisms on alcoholic liver damage are still fully unclear.

This work investigated the potential of LGG@Se in alleviating alcohol-induced liver damage in mice, and explored its liver protective mechanism through intestinal microbiome and liver metabolome analysis. The possible relationship between the key intestinal bacteria and the biochemical phenotypes was revealed by correlation network analysis. This study lays the foundation for understanding the biological functions of LGG@Se, and provides a valuable reference for the development of novel Se-enriched probiotic supplements to the prevention of ALI.

sec.title: Materials and methods
Materials and reagents
sec.p: L. rhamnosus GG (LGG, ATCC 53103) was stored in the Institute of Food Science and Technology (Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, China). Sodium selenite (Na2SeO3) was purchased from National Medicines Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). The antioxidant and biochemical assay kits for serum and liver samples were sourced from Elabscience® Biotech. Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China). The Mann, Rogosa, Sharp (MRS) broth medium was purchased by Chuangsai Biotech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The primary antibodies of low-density lipoprotein receptor (LDLr) (#ab52818), carnitine palmitoyltransferase-1 (CPT-1) (#ab234111), nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf-2) (#ab92946), heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) (#ab52947), Class II alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH2) #ab108203), Zona Occludens 1 (ZO-1) (#ab96587), Occludin (#ab222691) and Claudin-1 (#ab307692) for Western Blotting (WB) were obtained from Abcam Co., Ltd (Cambridge, UK). The primary antibody of glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px) (sc-133245) for Western Blotting (WB) were obtained from Santa Cruz Biotechnology (California, USA). β-actin (#YT0099) and HRP-conjugated anti-rabbit secondary antibody (#RS0002) were purchased from Ruiying Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Unless otherwise noted, this study utilized analysis-grade chemicals acquired from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Foshan, Guangdong, China).

sec.title: Preparation of Se-enriched L. rhamnosus GG
sec.p: The preparation process of Se-enriched L. rhamnosus GG (LGG@Se) was slightly modified on the basis of a previous report (Xu et al., 2019). Briefly, L. rhamnosus GG ATCC 53103 strain was first cultured in MRS broth for 24 h at 37 °C. Seed suspension from cultures of L. rhamnosus GG ATCC53103 (third generation) was harvested, and the bacterial solution with OD600 = 0.6–0.8 was added to the liquid medium (MRS broth) at an inoculum of 2% (v/v) for 6 h at 37 °C, then sodium selenite solution was introduced into sterilized MRS medium (final concentration 10 μg/mL), and then the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to obtain selenized L. rhamnosus GG (LGG@Se). Meanwhile, L. rhamnosus GG was also cultured in the absence of sodium selenite at 37 °C for 24 h to obtain non-selenized L. rhamnosus GG (LGG). After 24 h of cultivation, LGG@Se and LGG cells were obtained by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min). The LGG@Se and LGG were pre-frozen at −80 °C overnight, which were freeze-dried with a vacuum freeze-dryer, vacuum-packed and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use. LGG@Se and LGG bacterial solutions were prepared by redissolving lyophilized LGG@Se and LGG bacterial powder samples (0.1 g) in sterile saline (1 ml). The viable bacteria contents of LGG@Se and LGG solutions were adjusted to 5 × 109 CFU/mL by sterile saline, because we wanted to ensure that each mouse received 1 × 109 CFU of viable bacteria per day (gavage dose of 0.2 mL for mice weighing 40 g).

sec.title: Characterization of Se-enriched L. rhamnosus GG
sec.p: The Se contents of the dried L. rhamnosus GG and Se-enriched L. rhamnosus GG samples were analyzed through ICP-OES system (Agilent 5800, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The effect of Se-enriched culture on the surface morphology of LGG cells was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (JSM-6390LA, EX-2300, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The pre-treatment method of bacterial samples can refer to the method described in a previous study (Sun et al., 2022). L. rhamnosus GG (LGG) and Se-enriched L. rhamnosus GG (LGG@Se) were also characterized in fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Vetex70, Bruker, Germany) at 400–4000 cm−1, to observe the changes of functional groups of biological macromolecules in LGG after Se-enriched culture.

sec.title: Animals and experimental design
sec.p: Sixty Kunming mice (6 weeks old, male, specific pathogen-free) were obtained from Beijing HFK Bioscience Co., Ltd (Beijing, China), and raised under the standard environment (22 ± 1 °C temperature, 50–55% humidity, 12 h light/dark cycle) with ad libitum water and food. All experimental procedures were carried out in accordance with the relevant norms and received approval from the ethics committee of the workplace (approval No.: FZU-IFST-2022010). After the seven-day acclimatization, the mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 10), namely Control, Model, PC, LGG, LGG@Se and Se groups, respectively. The construction of mouse model with ALI was referred to a previous study (Wu et al., 2022a). Mice of the Model, PC, LGG, LGG@Se, and Se groups received oral gavage of 50% alcohol (v/v, 7.5 mL/kg b.w.) daily, 4 h after the administration of 0.2 mL physiological saline, silymarin (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) (100 mg/kg/day), LGG (109 CFU/day), LGG@Se (109 CFU/day) or inorganic Se (80.0 μg/kg/day). The daily intake of selenium in mice was based on the recommended daily intake for humans as well as drug-dose conversions between humans and animals (Genchi et al., 2023). The probiotic gavage regimen and dosage were mainly based on the regimen and dosage previously reported by Lin et al. (2020). The gavage amount of silymarin was mainly referred to the dosage previously reported by Wu et al. (2022a) and Aghaei et al. (2023). Mice in the Control group were given an equivalent volume of physiological saline. The mice were treated daily for six weeks. The weight of each mouse was measured and documented weekly during the course of the experiment. Following 42 days of intervention, all mice were fasted overnight and then euthanized under anesthesia. Blood samples were allowed to stand at room temperature for 4h and then centrifuged (3000 rpm) for at 25 °C for 10 min. The obtained serum was kept in sterile centrifuge tube at −20 °C for biochemical detection. Upon dissection, the liver, spleen, kidney, and colon of each mouse were weighed. The organ index (%) was calculated by organ weight (g)/mouse weight (g) × 100%. The liver and cecal contents were quickly frozen in liquid nitrogen and then stored at −80 °C for subsequent analysis.

sec.title: Biochemical analysis of serum and liver
sec.p: Biochemical assay kits (Elabscience®, Wuhan, China) were used to measure the levels of total cholesterol (TC), triglycerides (TG), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) in the serum. In addition, liver sample was homogenized with physiological saline. The supernatant was collected by high-speed centrifugation (10000×g for 10 min, 4 °C). Commercial test kits (Jiancheng, Nanjing, China) were utilized to determine the activities of liver catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px), alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH), and aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) activities.

sec.title: Histopathological analysis of the liver and jejunum
sec.p: Liver and jejunum tissue (approximately 10 mm3) were obtained and preserved in 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 24 h, followed by dehydration in a series of ethanol solutions. The specimens were embedded in paraffin blocks and subsequently sliced into sections with a thickness of 4 μm. The sections underwent deparaffinization, xylene clearing, and rehydration in a series of descending ethanol concentrations. Subsequently, observation of the hematoxylin–eosin (H&E) stained sections was carried out through an optical microscope equipped with a digital camera system (Nikon, Tokyo, Japan). Liver pathology scoring was assessed independently by three professionally trained personnel based on a previously reported study (Hu et al., 2020). Jejunum pathology scoring was also evaluated independently by three professionally trained personnel according to intestinal structure and inflammatory cell infiltration (Erben et al., 2014).

sec.title: Determination of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)
sec.p: Feces were gathered and freeze-dried, and then measured for weight. Following this, the freeze-dried feces were combined with a saturated NaCl solution and left to sit at room temperature for 30 min. Sulfuric acid (5 μL) was added to the sample solution, and then homogenized. After centrifugation (12000 rpm, 4 °C, 10 min), transferred the supernatant to the centrifuge tube (1.5 mL) with 0.8 mL of C2H5OC2H5 and 0.2 g anhydrous Na2SO4, and placed at room temperature for 30 min. After centrifugation (12000 rpm, 4 °C, 10 min), the SCFAs content was detected by Agilent 7890A gas chromatograph equipped with a hydrogen flame ionization detector and Agilent J&W CP-Wax 57 CB capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm). The operating parameters were in accordance with the method described in our previous study (Guo et al., 2020).

sec.title: High throughput sequencing of 16S rDNA amplicon
sec.p: Bacterial genomic DNA was extracted from feces using a fecal bacterial DNA extraction kit (MoBio, Carlsbad, CA, USA). The bacterial 16S rRNA gene V3-V4 hypervariable regions were amplified using 341F-806R universal primers. The PCR products were separated and purified using 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis and DNA Recovery Kit (Beckman Coulter, Indianapolis, IN), respectively. Finally, the concentration of the purified PCR products were measured using a NanoDrop ND-1000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The sample was conducted utilizing high-throughput sequencing technology on Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform at Majorbio Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) in accordance with our prior research protocol (Huang et al., 2018). QIIME 2.0 software (version 2019.7) was used for quality control and cluster analysis of the sequencing data, and the refined sequencing data (filtered sequences) were matched against the sequences within the GreenGenes database (Ver. 13.8) to characterize the bacterial phylotypes present in the intestines at the genus level (with more than 97% similarity). Genus-level variations between the different experimental groups were analyzed and visualized through STAMP software (version 2.1.3) based on the Welsh t-test. Spearman's correlation analysis was employed to examine the association between intestinal bacteria and biochemical parameters, and visualized through R program (ver. 4.1.2, pheatmap package) and Cytoscape (ver. 3.10.1).

sec.title: Non-targeted metabolomics analysis of the liver
sec.p: Liver tissues (50 mg) were extracted using 1 mL organic solvent extract (acetonitrile: methanol: water = 2:2:1) with internal standard of isotope (2 μg/mL L-2-chlorophenylalanine) through ultrasonic crushing for 10 min at 4 °C, and then centrifuged for 10 min (12000×g, 4 °C). The collected supernatants (400 μL) were transferred to a centrifuge tube and desiccated under vacuum at 30 °C. In order to increase the concentration of the liver metabolites, the concentrates were resuspended in 80% methanol (200 μL). The suspensions were passed through 0.22 μm microfilters before being subjected to analysis using ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (1290 Infinity UPLC, Agilent, CA, USA) and 6530 QTOF electrospray ionization mass spectrometer (Agilent, CA, USA) with an UPLC-BEH amide column (2.1 mm × 100 mm, 1.7 μm). Quality control (QC) samples were prepared by mixing equal volumes of all samples. Original data were preliminary analyzed using MPP software (Agilent, CA, USA). Liver metabolic profiling was analyzed through Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) using MetaboAnalyst 5.0. Additionally, orthogonal partial least-squares discrimination analysis (OPLS-DA) and volcano plot were used to identify liver metabolites with significant variations between the Model group and the LGG@Se group. Pathway analysis of liver biomarkers was conducted utilizing MetaboAnalyst 5.0.

sec.title: Quantitative reverse transcription PCR (qRT-PCR)
sec.p: RNA extraction from mouse liver was performed using a miRNeasy mini kit (Vazyme, Nanjing, China), and the extracted RNA were determined by a nucleic acid quantifier (Implen, MUC, Germany). Then the mRNA was reverse-transcribed into cDNA using a commercial kit (Takara, Beijing, China) containing gDNA scavenger. qPCR was performed in GeneLight 9820 quantitative PCR system (Anpuli Biological Engineering Co., Ltd., Xiamen, China) using SYBR® FAST qPCR Master Mix (Vazyme, Nanjing, China) with primers targeting specific genes. The transcription levels of all target genes were normalized using 18S rRNA gene. The qPCR data were assessed utilizing the standard 2−ΔΔCT method for analysis. The PCR primers used in this study are shown in Table S1.

sec.title: Western blot assay
sec.p: Liver and colon samples (50 mg) were homogenized at high speed in RIPA lysis buffer (pH 7.4) containing protease inhibitors and phosphatase inhibitors (Mu et al., 2012). After being fully homogenized by a homogenizer, the lysates were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The protein concentration in the supernatant was determined using a BCA protein assay kit (Tiangen, Chengdu, China). Identical amounts of proteins (50 μg) were loaded and separated on SDS-PAGE gels, and then transferred to polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane. The membrane was blocked with PBS-Tween 20 supplemented with 2.5% skim milk for 60 min at 25 °C. The membrane was incubated with the primary antibody overnight at 4 °C, followed by washing with PBS buffer and further incubated with an HRP-labeled secondary antibody for 60 min at room temperature. The protein bands were incubated with an enhanced chemiluminescence kit for colour development and photographed using an ImageQuant LAS 4000 mini system (GE Healthcare, USA). The optical density of the target protein band was quantitated and calculated using Image J software (NIH, Bethesda, MD, USA). The optical density of LDLr, CPT1, Nrf2, HO-1, GSH-Px, ADH2, ZO-1, Occludin, Claudin1 and GAPDH bands was normalized with the optical density of β-actin.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The physiological and biochemical test results of animal experiments were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed by GraphPad Prism 9 software. # indicates that compared with the Control group, p < 0.05; ∗ indicates that compared with the Model group, p < 0.05; ## indicates compared with the Control group, p < 0.01; ∗∗ indicates comparison with Model group, p < 0.01.

sec.title: Results
Characterization of LGG@Se by SEM-EDX and FTIR spectra
sec.p: As shown in Supplementary Materials-Fig. S1A, the LGG cells without selenium enrichment were morphologically complete and plump, and no other substances were observed. In contrast, the formation of nanoscale particles was observed on the surface of LGG cells after co-culture with sodium selenite, which could be attributed to the formation of zero-valence nano-Se. To prove this hypothesis, element analysis of nanoparticles produced on the surface of LGG cells was carried out by EDX analyzer. As shown in Fig. S1B of Supplementary Materials, the characteristic energy peak of zero-valence Se was detected near 1319 keV and 1388 keV, further indicating that Se-enriched LGG can form extracellular selenium nanoparticles (Deng et al., 2015). Besides, the prepared LGG@Se and LGG were also characterized in Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Figure S1C, the FTIR spectra of LGG@Se and LGG were similar, indicating that selenium nanoparticles can be formed on the surface of LGG cells by Se-enriched culture without damaging normal cell morphology and chemical functional groups. The peak at 3277 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H in the sugar residues present. Additionally, the peak at 2924 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-H within the sugar ring, and the peak at 1638 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C-O. The absorption peak at 1032 cm−1 indicated the α-pyranose form of the glucosyl residues. The peak at 888 cm−1 suggested the symmetrical stretching vibration of α-isomeric pyranose (Cheng et al., 2017).

sec.title: Effect of LGG@Se on body weight and organ indexes in over-drinking mice
sec.p:
As shown in Fig. 1A, before gavage intervention, there was no statistically significant difference in body weight among the experimental groups (p > 0.05), suggesting that the mice in all experimental groups started with similar initial body weights. Nevertheless, following a 6-week period of experimentation, the body weight of mice treated with alcohol was notably lower compared to that of mice not exposed to alcohol (p < 0.01) (Fig. 1B), indicating that alcohol exposure would affect the health condition and inhibit the weight gain of mice. Oral supplementation with both LGG@Se and silymarin was effective in preventing abnormal weight loss caused by excessive alcohol consumption (p < 0.05). In contrast, supplementation with non-selenium-enriched LGG with the same number of viable bacteria did not significantly improve weight abnormalities due to alcohol exposure (p > 0.05). Conversely, inorganic selenium (Se) intervention significantly exacerbated the weight loss caused by alcohol exposure (p < 0.01). In addition to body weight, organ index also reflects the damage of alcohol to the health condition to a certain extent. As shown in Fig. 1C–E, the liver and kidney indexes of mice in the Model group were significantly elevated compared to those in the Control group (p < 0.05), while the spleen index was notably lower than that of the Control group (p < 0.01). However, compared with the Model group, LGG@Se, LGG, and silymarin interventions all significantly alleviated the abnormalities of liver, kidney, and spleen indices due to alcohol exposure, and LGG@Se intervention was better than the equivalent dose of LGG intervention in alleviating the abnormalities of kidney and spleen indices. Interestingly, the intervention of inorganic selenium (Se) did not lead to a significant improvement in the kidney and spleen indexes of mice with excessive alcohol consumption (p > 0.05). These results indicated that LGG@Se has a better regulatory effect than LGG and inorganic selenium on the body weight and organ indexes in mice exposed to excessive alcohol.Fig. 1
Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on the body weight gain, liver index, kidney index and spleen index in over-drinking mice. (A) weight gain curve; (B) weight gain; (C) liver index; (D) kidney index; (E) spleen index. Compared with the Control group, 0.01 < #p < 0.05 and ##p < 0.01; compared with the Model group, 0.01 < ∗p < 0.05 and ∗∗p < 0.01. The number of mice in each experimental group was 10 (n = 10). Abbreviation description: LGG: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG; LGG@Se: Selenium-enriched Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG; Se: Inorganic selenium; PC: Positive control.Fig. 1

sec.title: Effect of LGG@Se on serum biochemical parameters in over-drinking mice
sec.p:
It is generally accepted that alcohol overconsumption can lead to lipid metabolism disorders and hepatocyte damage. In this study, excessive alcohol intake resulted in elevated serum levels of TC, TG, and LDL-C, along with a significant reduction in serum HDL-C level (p < 0.05) (Fig. 2). Interestingly, LGG@Se intervention significantly reduced alcohol-induced abnormal levels of serum TC, TG, LDL-C levels, and significantly increased serum HDL-C level (p < 0.05), with improvements comparable to or even superior to silymarin (p < 0.05). However, inorganic selenium intervention only significantly reduced serum LDL-C concentration (p < 0.05), but had no significant improvement on serum levels of TC, TG, and HDL-C in mice with excessive alcohol consumption. (p > 0.05). Besides, there was no significant difference in serum TC and TG levels between the Model and LGG groups (p > 0.05). The activities of serum ALT and AST indicate damage to hepatocyte cell membranes and mitochondria to a certain extent. Compared to Control group, mice with alcohol-induced liver injury exhibited elevated activities of serum AST and ALT. LGG@Se intervention significantly reduced serum activities of AST and ALT (p < 0.05), suggesting a certain degree of alleviation in alcoholic liver injury. The effect of LGG@Se on reducing serum AST activity was apparently better than that of LGG at the same dose. Together, these results suggested that LGG@Se was effective in alleviating dyslipidaemia and hepatocyte damage induced by alcohol exposure.Fig. 2
Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on the serum biochemical parameters in over-drinking mice. (A) serum TC; (B) serum TG; (C) serum LDL-C; (D) serum HDL-C; (E) serum AST; (F) serum ALT. Compared with the Control group, 0.01 < #p < 0.05 and ##p < 0.01; compared with the Model group, 0.01 < ∗p < 0.05 and ∗∗p < 0.01. The number of mice in each experimental group was 10 (n = 10). Abbreviation description: TC: total cholesterol; TG: triglycerides; LDL-C: low-density lipoprotein cholesterol; HDL-C: high-density lipoprotein cholesterol; AST: aspartate aminotransferase; ALT: alanine aminotransferase.Fig. 2

sec.title: Effect of LGG@Se on liver biochemical parameters in over-drinking mice
sec.p:
The progression of ALI is intricately linked to the generation of free radicals triggered by ethanol and its by-products. The level of oxidative stress in liver is regarded as an important index to evaluate the degree of alcoholic liver injury. As shown in Fig. 3, after 6 weeks of animal experiment, excessive alcohol intake induced abnormal reductions in hepatic activities of oxidative stress-related enzymes (including CAT, SOD and GSH-Px) (p < 0.05), and a significant decrease in hepatic ADH activity, suggesting that excessive alcohol exposure stimulated oxidative stress and destroyed metabolism function of liver. After 6 weeks of intervention with LGG, LGG@Se and Se, the activities of CAT, SOD and GSH-Px, ADH and ALDH in the liver of over-drinking mice were significantly increased (p < 0.05) compared with the Model group, and the activities of antioxidant enzymes in the liver other than SOD were also significantly elevated after silymarin treatment (p < 0.05), indicating that the mouse model of alcoholic liver injury constructed in this study was successful, and the prepared LGG@Se has excellent effect on improving alcoholic liver injury. Interestingly, LGG@Se intervention was more effective in elevating hepatic SOD and GSH-Px than LGG intervention with the equivalent dose. Besides, there was no significant difference in hepatic SOD and GSH-Px activities between the Model and PC groups, although silymarin treatment can significantly elevate the hepatic CAT, ADH and ALDH activities in mice with alcohol over-consumption. Collectedly, these results indicate that LGG@Se is helpful to relieve the oxidative stress induced by alcohol exposure, and its regulatory effect is apparently better than that of LGG and Se with the same dose.Fig. 3
Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on the liver biochemical parametersin over-drinking mice. (A) liver CAT; (B) liver SOD; (C) liver GSH-Px; (D) liver ADH; (E) liver ALDH. Compared with the Control group, 0.01 < #p < 0.05 and ##p < 0.01; compared with the Model group, 0.01 < ∗p < 0.05 and ∗∗p < 0.01. The number of mice in each experimental group was 10 (n = 10). Abbreviation description: CAT: catalase; SOD: superoxide dismutase; GSH-Px: glutathione peroxidase; ADH: alcohol dehydrogenase; ALDH:aldehyde dehydrogenase.Fig. 3

sec.title: Effects of LGG@Se on histopathological characteristics of liver and intestine
sec.p:
Pathological analysis of liver tissue sections by H&E staining showed that hepatocyte necrosis, nuclear shrinkage, inflammatory cell infiltration occurred in mice of the model group (Fig. 4A and B). While silymarin, LGG, LGG@Se and Se treatments obviously improved the arrangement and morphology of hepatocytes, among which silymarin and LGG@Se treatments had the best effect on morphological damage of hepatocytes. Additionally, jejunum H&E staining was used for histological analysis of jejunum samples. The impact of LGG, LGG@Se, and Se on the intestinal barrier integrity was observed through jejunum histopathological analysis (Fig. 4C and D). The results showed that excessive alcohol intake obviously damaged the intestinal mucosa, destroyed glandular structure, shortened crypts, and reduced the number of goblet cells. On the contrary, the histopathological features of jejunum in mice of the experimental groups, including PC group, LGG group, LGG@Se group and Se group, were obviously ameliorated.Fig. 4
Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on the liver and jejunum histopathological features in over-drinking mice. (A) H&E staining of liver; (B) liver pathology score; (C) H&E staining of jejunum; (D) jejunum pathology score. The number of mice in each experimental group was 10 (n = 10).Fig. 4

sec.title: Effect of LGG@Se on fecal SCFAs levels in over-drinking mice
sec.p:
SCFAs is a major metabolite produced by intestinal microbiota and exerts crucial physiological effects on the “gut-liver” axis. As can be seen from Fig. 5, compared to the Control group, exposure to alcohol significantly elevated the level of fecal acetic acid but reduced the levels of fecal propionic acid, n-butyric acid, and isobutyric acid in mice. (p < 0.01). It is worth mentioning that silymarin intervention remarkably recovered fecal SCFAs levels compared with the Model group (p < 0.01). The intervention with LGG effectively decreased the level of fecal acetic acid but increased the level of fecal propionic acid (p < 0.05), while the intervention with inorganic selenium significantly reduced the level of fecal acetic acid but elevated the level of fecal n-butyric acid (p < 0.05). In addition, the intervention of LGG@Se significantly reduced the level of fecal acetic acid and increased the level of fecal propionic and n-butyric acids (p < 0.05). Taken together, these results suggest that interventions with LGG, LGG@Se, and Se can reduce the abnormal increase of fecal acetic acid level caused by excessive alcohol consumption, especially LGG@Se intervention.Fig. 5
Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on the fecal SCFAs contents in over-drinking mice. (A) acetic acid; (A) propionic acid; (A) n-butyric acid; (A) iso-butyric acid. Compared with the Control group, 0.01 < #p < 0.05 and ##p < 0.01; compared with the Model group, 0.01 < ∗p < 0.05 and ∗∗p < 0.01. The number of mice in each experimental group was 10 (n = 10). Abbreviation description: SCFAs:short-chain fatty acids.Fig. 5

sec.title: Effects of LGG@Se on intestinal microbiota in over-drinking mice
sec.p:
For a long time, intestinal flora has been regarded as the "second genome" of mammals, which plays an irreplaceable role in maintaining body health. Exposure to alcohol not only causes liver damage but also affects intestinal microbiota composition. Intestinal bacterial disorder is one of the most important factors that aggravate the pathological progression of ALI. Therefore, we conducted fecal 16S amplicons sequencing to investigate intestinal microbiota composition altered by alcohol and the beneficial effects of selenium-enriched probiotics on restoring the balance of alcohol-disrupted intestinal flora. Compared with alcohol-free mice (the Control group), the abundances of Faecalibaculum (ASV13), Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group (ASV70, 117, 265, 295), Turicibacter (ASV7), Enterococcus (ASV276), Staphylococcus (ASV287), Muribaculum (ASV156), Blautia (ASV246) and Christensenellaceae R-7 group (ASV209) showed significant increases in over-drinking mice (the Model group), but the abundances of Lactobacillus (ASV443), Bacillus (ASV10, 1240), Aerococcus (ASV1114), Staphylococcus (ASV526), Jeotgalibaca (ASV1116), Jeotgalicoccus (ASV811), Lachnospiraceae UCG-001 (ASV650), Facklamia (ASV1119) and Butyricicoccus (ASV1135) were significantly reduced (Fig. 6A). However, LGG intervention effectively elevated the abundances of Bifidobacterium (ASV519), unclassified_f_Atopobiaceae (ASV22), Lachnospiraceae UCG-006 (ASV49), Enterorhabdus (ASV185), Ruminococcus (ASV188) and Lactobacillus (ASV396, 621) compared with the Model group, while significantly reduced the relative abundance of unclassified_f_Lachnospiraceae (ASV 26, 37, 66, 79, 86), Candidatus Saccharimonas (ASV38), Lactobacillus (ASV170) and Alistipes (ASV291, 310) (Fig. 6B). Interestingly, LGG@Se intervention significantly increased the relative abundances of Clostridia_UCG-014 (ASV25, 392), Eubacterium siraeum group (ASV36), Eubacterium xylanophilum group (ASV44), Lachnospiraceae NK4A136_group (ASV125), Adlercreutzia (ASV76), Ruminococcus (ASV188), Eubacterium xylanophilum_group (ASV448), Lactobacillus (ASV396), norank_f_Muribaculaceae (ASV540), Lachnospiraceae UCG-001 (ASV650) and Faecalibacterium UBA1819 (ASV846) compared with the Model group, but remarkably reduced the relative abundances of unclassified_f_Lachnospiraceae (ASV213) and Enterorhabdus (ASV34) (Fig. 6C). Inorganic selenium treatment remarkably decreased the relative abundances of Desulfovobrio (ASV5), Lachnospiraceae UCG-006 (ASV27), Desulfovobrio (ASV16), Candidatus Saccharimonas (ASV94, 266), and Enterorhabdus (ASV29, 106) in over-drinking mice, but remarkably increased the relative abundance of Eubacterium ruminantium group (ASV535), Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group (ASV223), Muribaculum (ASV156), Bacteroides (ASV203, 233, 229, 557, 422), Rikenellaceae RC9 gut group (ASV167), Alistipes (ASV105, 310, 254, 630), Ruminococcus (ASV188), Helicobacter (ASV224), Rikenella (ASV241), Paludicola (ASV249) and Ruminococcaceae NK4A214 group (ASV602) (Fig. S2).Fig. 6
The differences in the relative abundances of intestinal bacterial genera between the Model and Control, LGG and LGG@Se groups. Bars on the left represent the proportion (%) of bacterial genera, and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR corrected p-values less than 0.05 indicates statistically significant differences (∗p < 0.05). (A) The Control group versus the Model group; (B) The LGG group versus the Model group; (C) The LGG@Se group versus the Model group. The number of mice in each experimental group was 10 (n = 10).Fig. 6

The visualization of the correlations between intestinal bacteria and biochemical indicators was achieved through heatmap and network. As shown in Fig. 7, the fecal acetate, serum LDL-C was positively associated with Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group (ASV70, 295), Blautia (ASV246), Enterorhabdus (ASV34), unclassified_f_Lachnospiraceae (ASV213), but negatively associated with Bacillus (ASV10). Serum AST was positively associated with Faecalibaculum (ASV13), unclassified_f_Lachnospiraceae (ASV107), Turicibacter (ASV7), but negatively associated with Lachnospiraceae UCG-001 (ASV650), unclassifed_c_Bacilli (ASV1239), Bacillus (ASV1240), Lactobacillus (ASV443), Staphylococcus (ASV526), Butyricicoccus (ASV1135) and Jeotgalicoccus (ASV811). Serum TG was positively associated with Muribaculum (ASV156), Blautia (ASV246), Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group (ASV117), Faecalibacterium UBA1819 (ASV846), Lactobacillus (ASV396), but negatively associated with Lactobacillus (ASV443) and Staphylococcus (ASV526). Liver SOD was positively associated with Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group (ASV117), Lactobacillus (ASV396), norank_f_Eubacterium coprostanoligenes group (ASV510), norank_f_Muribaculaceae (ASV540), Faecalibacterium UBA1819 (ASV846), Eubacterium siraeum group (ASV36). Liver GSH-Px was positively associated with unclassified_f_Lachnospiraceae (ASV456), but negatively associated with Enterorhabdus (ASV34), unclassified_f_Lachnospiraceae (ASV172). It is worth noting that fecal SCFAs including n-butyrate, propionate and isobutyrate were negatively associated with Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group (ASV70, 265), Faecalibaculum (ASV13), Enterococcus (ASV276), Turicibacter (ASV7), Muribaculum (ASV156), Enterorhabdus (ASV34), norank_o_Clostridia_UCG-014 (ASV25), Adlercreutzia (ASV76), Ruminococcus (ASV188) and Eubacterium siraeum group (ASV36), but positively associated with Lachnospiraceae UCG-001 (ASV650), Bacillus (ASV10, 1240), unclassified_c_Bacilli (ASV1239), Lactobacillus (ASV443), Staphylococcus (ASV526), Butyricicoccus (ASV1135), Jeotgalicoccus (ASV811), Facklamia (ASV1119), Aerococcus (ASV1114) and Jeotgalibaca (ASV1116). These findings suggest strong correlations between the key intestinal bacteria regulated by LGG@Se and its beneficial effects on mitigating alcoholic liver injury, suggesting that the modulation of intestinal bacteria may be one of the pathways by which LGG@Se reverses alcohol-induced liver injury.Fig. 7
Spearman's correlations between the key intestinal microbes and biochemical parameters. (A) Heatmap of correlation coefficients. Note: The blue square is a negative correlation, the red square is a positive correlation, ∗∗p < 0.01 and ∗∗p < 0.05 indicate extremely significant correlation or significant correlation, respectively. (B) Visualization of the correlation network. Red nodes: the key microbes increased by LGG@Se intervention; green nodes: the key microbes reduced by LGG@Se intervention; blue nodes: the biochemical parameters. The solid red line and the black line represent positive and negative correlation, respectively. Line widthes represent the strength of correlation. Only the significant edges were drawn in the network (|r| > 0.6, ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01).Fig. 7

sec.title: Effects of LGG@Se on liver metabolomic profiling in over-drinking mice
sec.p:
One step further, investigate the changes in liver metabolism of mice after LGG@Se treatment regarding excessive drinking, untargeted metabolomics using UPLC-QTOF/MS was utilized to analyze liver metabolites in the Control, Model, LGG@Se, and Se groups. To further explore the alteration of liver metabolism in over-drinking mice after LGG@Se treatment, untargeted metabolomics was applied to analyze the liver metabolites in mice of the Control, Model, LGG@Se and Se groups. Principal component analysis (PCA) is essentially an “unsupervised mode” multivariate statistical analysis in which the differences between samples/groups are analyzed directly. As shown in Fig. 8, Fig. 9A, the scores plots of PCA for liver metabolites distinctly separated the Se group from other experimental groups (including the Control, Model, LGG@Se groups) both in ESI + and ESI- modes, while the Control, Model and LGG@Se groups were closely linked together, indicating that oral inorganic selenium treatment may have a very obvious effect on the liver metabolic function of mice. Partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) and orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA) are “supervised mode” multivariate statistical analysis that exclude unnecessary intra-group errors/biases and generally better analyze the inter-group differences. In the PLS-DA score plots of liver metabolites, there was a clear separation between the Control, Model, LGG@Se, and Se groups (Fig. 8, Fig. 9B), suggesting that oral administration of LGG@Se and Se effectively changed the composition of liver metabolites in mice with excessive alcohol consumption. To further explore the beneficial impact of LGG@Se on liver metabolism in mice, OPLS-DA of liver metabolites between the model group and the LGG@Se group was conducted. According to Fig. 8, Fig. 9C, the LGG@Se group clearly separated from the Model group, suggesting that LGG@Se may regulate liver metabolic disorders induced by alcohol exposure. In the ESI + mode, a total of 67 distinct metabolites were identified between the Model and LGG@Se groups, with 24 liver metabolites up-regulated and 43 down-regulated (Fig. 8D). These differential metabolites were primarily associated with nine metabolic pathways including glyoxylate and dicarboxylate metabolism, steroid hormone biosynthesis, sphingolipid metabolism, thiamine metabolism, tryptophan metabolism, arginine and proline metabolism, alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism, arginine biosynthesis, and D-glutamine and D-glutamate metabolism (Fig. 8E). The analysis results of ESI- mode revealed that a total of 61 differential metabolites were identified between the Model and LGG@Se groups were screened, including the up-regulation of 21 liver metabolites and the down-regulation of 40 liver metabolites (Fig. 9D). These differential metabolites were enriched in purine metabolism, alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism, nitrogen metabolism, pyrimidine metabolism, D-glutamine and D-glutamate metabolism, arginine biosynthesis, ubiquinone and other terpenoid-quinone biosynthesis, phenylalanine, tyrosine and tryptophan biosynthesis, tyrosine metabolism, citrate cycle (TCA cycle), phenylalanine metabolism, and ascorbate and aldarate metabolism (Fig. 9E).Fig. 8
Liver metabolomic profiling by UPLC-QTOF/MS in the ESI + model. (A) PCA score plot for the Control, Model, LGG@Se, and Se groups; (B) PLS-DA score plot for the Control, Model, LGG@Se, and Se groups; (C) OPLS-DA score plot for the LGG@Se and Model groups; (D) S-loading plot based on the OPLS-DA analysis model of the LGG@Se and Model groups; (E) Heatmap of the relative abundance of significantly different metabolites (VIP value > 1.0, p < 0.05) between the Model and LGG@Se groups; (F) Metabolic pathway impact prediction based on the KEGG online database.Fig. 8Fig. 9
Liver metabolomic profiling by UPLC-QTOF/MS in the ESI- model. (A) PCA score plot for the Control, Model, LGG@Se, and Se groups; (B) PLS-DA score plot for the Control, Model, LGG@Se, and Se groups; (C) OPLS-DA score plot for the LGG@Se and Model groups; (D) S-loading plot based on the OPLS-DA analysis model of the LGG@Se and Model groups; (E) Heatmap of the relative abundance of significantly different metabolites (VIP value > 1.0, p < 0.05) between the Model and LGG@Se groups; (F) Metabolic pathway impact prediction based on the KEGG online database.Fig. 9

sec.title: Effects of LGG@Se on liver gene transcription and protein expression levels
sec.p:
The liver plays a pivotal role in lipid metabolism, bile acid biosynthesis, and alcohol metabolism. To investigate the mechanism by which the LGG@Se treatment protects against ALI, liver gene transcription and protein expression related to fatty acid metabolism, bile acid biosynthesis and alcohol metabolism were further analyzed by RT-qPCR and Western blot (Fig. 10). In the Model group, the transcription levels of liver genes including Ldlr, Cyp7a1, Cpt-1, Acsl1, Acox1, Ppar-α, ADH2, ALDH2, Nrf2, CAT and HO-1 were significantly decreased compared with the Control group (p < 0.05), while the gene transcription levels of liver CYP2E1 and Cd36 were significantly elevated in ALI mice induced by alcohol exposure (p < 0.05) (Fig. 10A). Interestingly, compared with the Model group, both of LGG and LGG@Se interventions effectively prevented alcohol-induced abnormal transcription changes in liver genes related to alcohol, fatty acid and cholesterol metabolism. Compared with the Model group, LGG intervention significantly increased the transcription levels of liver genes including Ldlr, Cyp7a1, Cpt-1, Acsl1, Acox1, Ppar-α, ADH2, ALDH2, HO-1, and significantly reduced the transcription levels of liver genes including CYP2E1 and CD36, (p < 0.05). However, Se intervention only effectively prevented alcohol-induced abnormal transcription changes of liver genes including Cyp7a1, Cpt-1, Acsl1, Acox1, ADH2, ALDH2, CAT and GSH-Px compared with the Model group (p < 0.05). LGG@Se was more effective than LGG in regulating the transcription of liver alcohol metabolism-related gene (Cpt-1, Acsl1, ADH2, ALDH2, Nrf2, CAT and GSH-Px), and more effective than inorganic Se in regulating the transcription of liver metabolism-related genes (Ldlr, Cyp7a1, Acsl1, Ppar-α and CAT). In addition, compared with the Model group, Western blot analysis showed that excessive drinking significantly decreased the expression of key proteins/enzymes (including Ldlr, Cpt1, Nrf2, HO-1, GSH-Px, ADH2, etc.) in the liver (p < 0.05) (Fig. 10B). It is noteworthy that LGG@Se treatment significantly elevated the protein expression levels of liver Ldlr, Cpt1, Nrf2, HO-1, GSH-Px and ADH2 in over-drinking mice (p < 0.05).Fig. 10
Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on gene transcription and protein expression in liver and intestine of mice with alcoholism. (A) Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on liver mRNA levels of genes related to fatty acid metabolism, alcohol metabolism and oxidative stress. (B) Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on liver protein (LDLr, CPT1, Nrf2, HO-1, GSH-Px and ADH2) expressions. (C) Effects of LGG, LGG@Se and Se intervention on the intestinl protein (ZO-1, Occludin and Claudin1) expression. Compared with the Control group, 0.01 < #p < 0.05 and ##p < 0.01; compared with the Model group, 0.01 < ∗p < 0.05 and ∗∗p < 0.01. Abbreviation description: Ldlr: low-density lipoprotein receptor; Cyp7a1: cholesterol 7 alpha-hydroxylase; Cpt-1: carnitine palmitoyltransferase-1; Acsl1: acyl-CoA synthetase long chain family member 1; Acox1: acyl-CoA oxidase 1; Pparα: peroxisome proliferator activated receptor alpha; CYP2E1: cytochrome P450 family 2 subfamily E member 1; ADH2: Class II alcohol dehydrogenase; ALDH2: aldehyde dehydrogenase 2 family member; Nrf-2: nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2; CAT: catalase; HO-1: heme oxygenase-1; SOD1: superoxide dismutase 1; GSH-Px: glutathione peroxidase; Cd36: fatty acid transposase; ZO-1: Zona Occludens 1.Fig. 10

sec.title: Effects of LGG@Se on intestinal barrier related protein expression levels
sec.p: Tight junctional proteins are major components for maintaining intestinal barrier function. Therefore, the regulatory effects of LGG and LGG@Se on the protein expression of ZO-1, Occludin and Claudin-1 in jejunal tissues of mice given excessive alcohol were analyzed by Western blotting (Fig. 10C). The results showed that excessive alcohol intake caused lower protein expression of colonic ZO-1, Occludin and Claudin-1, suggesting that alcohol exposure significantly elevated the permeability of the intestinal barrier. However, both LGG and LGG@Se treatments significantly promoted the expression of colonic protein ZO-1, Occludin and Claudin-1 in over-drinking mice, indicating that oral administration of Lf or Lf@Se is beneficial in preventing the damage of intestinal barrier caused by alcohol exposure, slowing down the development of alcohol-induced liver injury.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Selenium is essential for reducing oxidative stress in the body. Therefore, selenium supplementation is expected to reduce alcohol-induced liver damage by reducing oxidative stress. However, the modulatory effects of selenium-rich probiotics on liver metabolism and intestinal microbiota in mice with alcohol over-consumption remain unclear. In this study, we observed that oral administration of LGG@Se was effective in alleviating alcohol-induced lipid metabolism disorders and intestinal microbiota imbalance in mice, thereby significantly ameliorating ALI. Our comparative study demonstrated that LGG@Se was more effective in alleviating ALI compared with equivalent doses of LGG and sodium selenite.

Under normal physiological condition, AST and ALT are mainly distributed in the cytoplasm and the mitochondria of hepatocytes, respectively. When hepatocyte injury occurs, intracellular AST and ALT enter the bloodstream through the cell membrane, which in turn leads to abnormally high serum AST and ALT levels (Apte et al., 2004). Therefore, serum ALT and AST levels can be used to determine whether the liver is damaged and the extent of its damage (Khattab et al., 2015). As the most liver-specific cytoplasmic enzyme, serum ALT level is closely related to the damage of cell membrane. In addition, the abnormal increase in AST activity reflects the damage of hepatocyte mitochondria to a certain extent. Chronic excessive alcohol consumption has been reported to result in abnormally elevated serum ALT and AST levels, suggesting that alcohol exposure disrupts liver function (Wang et al., 2022). In this study, serum ALT and AST levels in mice of the Model group were abnormally elevated, suggesting that ALI mouse model was successfully established. Furthermore, a disturbance in lipid metabolism caused by alcohol is primarily characterized by abnormal serum levels of TC, TG, LDL-C, and HDL-C, aligning with the findings of a previous study (Ye et al., 2023). High levels of serum TC and TG are linked to increased blood viscosity, which is a significant factor in the risk of cardiovascular disease (Li et al., 2023). Evidence suggests that reducing serum LDL-C levels can decrease cardiovascular risk and play a role in preventing liver damage development (Pinkosky et al., 2016). HDL-C plays a crucial role in transporting cholesterol from different organs to the liver (Wang et al., 2010). In this study, it was discovered that the intervention with LGG@Se mitigates the abnormal levels of serum markers such as TC, TG, LDL-C, HDL-C, ALT, and AST that are caused by alcohol exposure. indicating that LGG@Se can effectively reduce the adverse effects of alcohol exposure on lipid metabolism and liver health. Compared to equal doses of LGG and Se, it is clear that LGG@Se is more advantageous in ameliorating alcohol-induced abnormalities in lipid metabolism and hepatic transaminase levels.

In the liver, alcohol is first oxidized to acetaldehyde under the catalysis of ADH, and then further decomposed to acetic acid under the catalysis of ALDH (Kim et al., 2018). Therefore, the simultaneous elevation of ADH and ALDH activities in the liver would favour the promotion of the oxidation and excretion of absorbed alcohol, thereby reducing its damage to the liver. Moreover, there is mounting evidence indicating that excessive alcohol consumption leads to oxidative stress damage in the liver, thus playing a significant role in the development of liver damage (Gao et al., 2019). Changes in the activities of antioxidant enzymes (e.g., GSH-Px, SOD and CAT) and the levels of lipid peroxidation products in the liver can indicate the state of oxidative stress in the liver, and are also important indicators of alcohol-induced liver injury (Zou et al., 2019). Antioxidant enzymes (CAT, SOD and GSH-Px) reduce the toxicity of free radicals to the liver. As an important antioxidant enzyme, liver SOD prevents liver function by mitigating the generation of reactive oxygen species and serves as a pivotal free radical scavenger in this process. GSH-Px and CAT are widely recognized as important antioxidant factors that reduce excess accumulation of peroxides in the liver by catalyzing hydrogen peroxide to produce water and oxygen (Li et al., 2016; Cao et al., 2021; He et al., 2022). Our study has showed that LGG, LGG@Se and Se interventions effectively elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes and alcohol metabolizing enzymes in the liver of excessive drinking mice to a certain extent, indicating that they have the ability to alleviate oxidative stress and enhance alcohol metabolism, thereby mitigating alcohol-induced liver injury. It is worth noting that the effect of LGG@Se is better than that of the same dose of LGG and Se, especially in increasing the activities of SOD and GSH-Px in liver.

The intestinal microbiota plays a crucial role in the host's metabolism of materials, immune defense mechanisms, growth regulation, and other vital life processes, and it is also closely linked to obesity, alcoholic liver injury, type 2 diabetes, constipation, and other pathological conditions (Clemente et al., 2012). It is particularly noteworthy that an imbalance in intestinal flora has been identified as a crucial factor in the development of ALI. In our prior research, it was discovered that excessive alcohol consumption led to an increase in the population of harmful bacteria and a decrease in the population of beneficial bacteria in the gut (Lv et al., 2022). Therefore, maintaining intestinal flora homeostasis may be one of the important and effective ways to prevent and treat ALI. In this study, exposure to alcohol resulted in a significant increase in the relative abundance of Enterococcus, Staphylococcus and Muribaculum compared to the Control group. As a resident bacterium in the host, the abundance of Enterococcus significantly increases in alcohol-drinking populations (Liu et al., 2022). One previous study alao showed that overgrowth of Enterococcus in the gut exacerbates the development of ethanol-induced liver injury by promoting hepatic inflammation and hepatocellular death, and thereby exacerbating the development of ethanol-induced liver injury (Llorente et al., 2017). Staphylococcus is a common pathogenic bacteria that can form biofilms, which often causes some diseases and is an important influencing factor for some morbidity and mortality in the world (Luther et al., 2015). The abundance of Muribaculum was reported to be strongly associated with the serum ALT and AST activities (Do et al., 2022). In our study, it was observed that LGG intervention had a significant impact on increasing the relative abundance of certain bacteria in mice that consume excessive alcohol. Specifically, the levels of Bifidobacterium, Lachnospiraceae UCG-006, Enterorhabdus, Ruminococcus and Lactobacillus increased considerably. Bifidobacterium, belonging to the phylum of Actinobacteria, has been reported to be able to decrease the body weight, serum TG, gross energy intake and visceral fat in high-fat diet-fed mice (Bo et al., 2020). Enterorhabdus and Ruminococcus were previously been reported to produce butyrate and propionate, both of which are beneficial for maintaining the integrity of intestinal barrier by promoting the expression of tight junctional proteins (Tu et al., 2021). In addition, Eubacterium xylanophilum group was previously reported to be able to produce SCFAs, which are strongly associated with the levels of IL-10, M-CSF, LBP and liver injury (Zhuge et al., 2021). Adlerkreutzia is a human intestinal bacterium, and its abundance is obviously reduced in CD patients (Čipčić Paljetak et al., 2022). Moreover, Ruminococcus is an important mucin-degrading bacterium and has been proven to reduce serum TC, TG and LDL-C, alleviate insulin resistance and inflammation, and thus improve energy metabolism (Wu et al., 2022b). As an important SCFAs-producing bacterium, Lachnospiraceae UCG-001 has been found to inhibit colon inflammation and tumor development (Guo and Li, 2019). Here, it was found that the relative abundances of Eubacterium xylanophilum group, Adlerkreutzia, Ruminococcus, and Lachnospiraceae UCG-001 increased significantly after the intervention of LGG@Se in over-drinking mice.

Previous studies have shown that over-drinking can cause an abnormal increase in intestinal permeability and intestinal endotoxin absorption, thereby inducing or accelerating ALI. The intestinal barrier is critical for maintaining intestinal homoeostasis and preventing harmful substances from entering the systemic circulation. In this study, ethanol exposure significantly decreased the expression of jejunum tight junction proteins (ZO-1, Occludin, and Claudin1) and damaged the structural integrity of jejunum tissue in mice, thereby disrupting intestinal barrier function. Among them, ZO-1 is the first tight-linking protein discovered in intestinal epithelial cells, which acts as a "bridge" between actin and transmembrane proteins (Poritz et al., 2007). A previous study showed that ZO-1 deficiency leads to increased intestinal barrier permeability and aggravates the severity of colitis (Kuo et al., 2021). Upregulated ZO-1 expression promotes the accumulation of e-cadherin, which plays an important role in the junction polarity of both monolayer and lamellar epithelium (Rübsam et al., 2017). Another function of ZO-1 determines the location of claudin-claudin aggregation between cells. The study results indicated that the expression levels of tight junction proteins in the intestinal tract of the LGG@Se group were notably higher compared to the Model group. The function of occludin is not fully understood, but some studies suggest that it plays a vital role in maintaining the structure and function of intestinal barrier. In addition, claudins acts as a major diffusion barrier for tight junctions and produces apical and basolateral roots. Therefore, we preliminarily speculate that LGG@Se may partially salvage ethanol-induced liver injury by improving intestinal barrier function (promoting the expression of ZO-1, Occludin, and Claudin1).

In liver metabolomics analysis, PCA and PLS-DA were used to demonstrate the characteristics of liver metabolic profiles of mice in different experimental groups. The samples of the LGG@Se and model group were obviously separated in the PCA and PLS-DA score plots, indicating that there were obvious differences in the characteristics of metabolite compositions in the LGG@Se group and the model group. To further explore the beneficial impact of LGG@Se on liver metabolism, we performed intergroup difference analysis of liver metabolites between the LGG@Se and Model groups using OPLS-DA. In this study, a total of 128 liver metabolites (biomarkers) with significant differences were identified between the model group and the LGG@Se group, of which 45 liver metabolites up-regulated after LGG@Se intervention, and 83 liver metabolites down-regulated by LGG@Se intervention. Among them, thiamin is an essential cofactor for important biochemical reactions in all living organisms. A previous study found that oral thiamine can improve liver metabolic disorders caused by di-(2-ethylhexyl)-phthalate (Fan et al., 2020). As an octadecanol, sphingosine has been demonstrated to effectively restrain both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria in both in vitro and in vivo studies. L-kynurenine is a central metabolite of tryptophan degradation and can inhibit colitis by inducing Treg cells (Tashita et al., 2020). As an 18-membered macrolide polyketide, borrelidin inhibits adipocyte differentiation by regulating the expression of GATA-3 (Matsuo et al., 2015). Costunolide is a natural sesquiterpene lactone compound that has a range of physiological activities, including anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory activities (Lv et al., 2021). 5′-AMP belongs to the class of organic compounds known as purine ribonucleoside monophosphatesthat increase cellular adenosine concentration, thereby reducing the expression of inflammatory genes (Zhan et al., 2014). Unsaturated fatty acids such as PE 40:7, PE 34:2 and PE 38:6 have been reported to be negatively associated with Parkinson's disease and lipid metabolic diseases (Fan et al., 2020). Chlorogenic acid is one of the most abundant phenolic acids and may be a potential candidate for improving energy metabolism by inhibiting elevated serum TC and TG levels (Ye et al., 2021). In the present study, LGG@Se intervention significantly increased the concentrations of thiamin, sphingosine, L-kynurenine, borrelidin, costunolide, 5′-AMP, PE, and chlorogenic acid in the livers of mice that consume excessive amounts of alcohol. Cycloheximide is an antibiotic derived from Streptomyces griseus. A previous study has suggested that cycloheximide intervention could induce acute hepatotoxicity (Al Nebaihi et al., 2022). Hypoxanthine is a reaction intermediate in the purine-degradation pathway. Hypoxanthine is widely used to establish the hyperuricemia model because excessive intake of purine can result in the overproduction of uric acid (Chen et al., 2021). L-homoarginine is an endogenous amino acid in many species that are related to cardiovascular diseases, stroke, and kidney function injury (Wetzel et al., 2019). Kynurenic acid is an endogenous tryptophan metabolite that may exacerbate cognitive dysfunction and memory impairment in Alzheimer's disease(Ostapiuk and Urbanska, 2022). Lithocholic acid is a monohydroxy bile acid produced by intestinal flora and was previously considered to be toxic. A recent study also exhibited that high concentrations of lithocholic acid induce oxidative stress and DNA damage, which accelerate the development of tumors (Nguyen et al., 2021). Homocitrulline is derived by carbamylation that are known to be associated with various diseases, such as atherosclerosis and liver function injury (Yang et al., 2021). Succinate functions as a major intermediate product of mitochondrial energy metabolism. Elevated levels of succinate have been identified in patients and mouse models with cardiovascular disease (Zhang et al., 2022). p-Cresol, a gut bacterial product of tyrosine catabolism, has been identified as a significant risk factor for cardiovascular mortality in patients with chronic kidney disease (Unno and Ichitani, 2022). In this study, liver metabolomics results indicated that the intervention with LGG@Se significantly decreased the levels of several compounds in the liver of ALI mice, including cycloheximide, hypoxanthine, L-homoarginine, lithocholic acid, homocitrulline, succinate, and p-cresol.

Excessive long-term alcohol consumption can result in disturbances of hepatic fatty acid metabolism, characterized by an increase in fatty acid synthesis and a decrease in fatty acid β-oxidation (Wang et al., 2022). From our study, LGG@Se intervention significantly inhibited the transcription of hepatic Cd36 gene, and promoted the transcription of hepatic Cyp7a1, Cpt-1, Acox1, Acsl1, and Ppar-alpha genes in excessive drinking mice. Among them, Cd36 (also known as fatty acid translocase) is a multifunctional protein that mainly acts as a fatty acid translocator to determine the transmembrane transport of long-chain fatty acids (Hao et al., 2020). Ppar-α, Acsl1 and Cpt-1 are closely related to the β-oxidation of fatty acids. Activation of Ppar-α favours the promotion of Acox 1 expression, which reduces fatty acid accumulation in the liver. Acsl1, distributed in the mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum of the liver, catalyses the production of acyl-coenzyme a from fatty acids which is then destined for β-oxidation (Huh et al., 2020). CPT-1 is a crucial rate-limiting factor in the β-oxidation process of long-chain fatty acids. In addition, several studies have explored the relationship between Cyp7a1 and liver-related lipid metabolism disorders (Carnuta et al., 2018). Cyp7a1 is the rate-limiting enzyme in the conversion of liver cholesterol to bile acids, which reduces the accumulation of cholesterol. Based on these previous findings and in combination with the results of this study, it can be speculated that LGG@Se may improve fatty acids synthesis and β-oxidation, as well as cholesterol decomposition, by regulating the expression of Cd36, Cyp7a1, Cpt-1, Acox1, Acsl1 and Ppar-α in liver of over-drinking mice, thus achieving the progression of prevention of ALI.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study is the first to investigate the ameliorative effect of LGG@Se on ALI and its potential mechanism of action in mice. We found that Se-enriched culture effectively enhanced the hepatoprotective effect of LGG. Besides, LGG@Se intervention significantly alleviated alcohol-induced lipid metabolism disorders and oxidative stress injury, intestinal microbiota disturbance, and intestinal mucosal barrier impairment. Furthermore, LGG@Se intervention modulated the expression of hepatic genes associated with lipid metabolism and oxidative stress in mice. Collectively, these findings revealed that selenium-enriched probiotics has stronger hepatoprotective efficiency than non-selenium rich probiotics. This study provides a new idea to enhance the bioactivity of probiotics to prevent ALI. In future study, the beneficial effects of selenium-enriched probiotics on ALI and the mechanism of action need to be verified by clinical population trials.
